1: BRANCH, Branch, or branch: nêtser Is Christ the branch (or nêtser) of Isaiah 11:1? There are only four references in the Hebrew Bible that use nêtser for branch. Of course, one is Isaiah 11:1, as shown above. The other three are as follows: Isaiah 14:19: In reference to Lucifer, its reads "But thou art cast out of thy grave like an abominable branch, and as the raiment of those that are slain, thrust through with a sword that go down to the stones of the pit; as a carcase (sic) (carcass) trodden under foot." Isaiah 60:21: Speaking about righteous Israel, the Lord states, "Thy people also shall be all righteous: they shall inherit the land for ever, the branch of my planting, the work of my hands, that I may be glorified." Daniel 11:7: In reference to a "king's daughter of the south" it reads, "But out of a branch of her roots shall one stand up in his estate which shall come with an army, and shall enter into the fortress of the king of the north, and shall deal against them, and shall prevail." It is noteworthy that not one of these other three uses of nêtser for branch is used in allusion to Christ. BRANCH, Branch or Tsemach as an Honorific Title for Christ: By contrast, many references to Christ as the Branch, a capitalized honorific title, are used in the Hebrew Bible. Pursuant to Strong's, such Branch is the Hebrew word tsemach (reference number 6780) and means "a sprout (usually concrete), literally or figuratively - branch, bud, that which (where) grew (upon), spring(-ing)". All the references in the Hebrew Bible that employ this use of tsemach are as follows: Isaiah 4:2: "In that day shall the branch of the Lord be beautiful and glorious and the fruit of the earth shall be excellent and comely for them that are escaped of Israel." Given that this version of the word tsemach is not capitalized by the KJV translators, biblical scholars have found dual denotations for it. According to Parry et al, "This word [tsemach] may have a double meaning. First, the Lord is called Branch messianically." (See below.) "Second, an offshoot of the house of Israel is often called "a righteous branch" (Jacob 2:25) or a "branch of the house of Israel" (1 Ne. 15:12; 2 Ne. 3:5) The context of this section suggests that the branch represents a specific remnant, or branch, of Israel that remains in Israel after God's judgments, identified in Isaiah 3." (Parry, Parry, and Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, pp. 47-48) (See also, Nyman, pp. 37 - 38) Jeremiah 23:5: "Behold, the days come, saith the Lord, that I will raise unto David a righteous Branch, and a King shall reign and prosper, and shall execute judgment and justice in the earth." All biblical scholars and authorities appear to agree that this instance of tsemach refers to Christ. Jeremiah 33:15: "In those days, and at that time, will I cause the Branch of righteousness to grow up unto David; and he shall execute judgment and righteousness in the land." All biblical scholars and authorities appear to agree that this instance of tsemach refers to Christ. Zechariah 3:8: "Hear now, O Joshua, the high priest, thou, and thy fellows that sit before thee: for they are men wondered at: for, behold, I will bring forth my servant the BRANCH." All Biblical scholars and authorities appear to agree that this instance of tsemach refers to Christ. Zechariah 6:12-13: "And speak unto him, saying, Thus speaketh the Lord of hosts, saying, Behold he man whose name is The BRANCH; and he shall grow up out of his lace, and he shall build the temple of the Lord. Even he shall build the temple of the Lord; and he shall bear the glory, and shall sit and rule upon his throne; and he shall be a priest upon his throne: and the counsel of peace shall be between them both." All biblical scholars and authorities appear to agree that this instance of tsemach refers to Christ. Equating Tsemach with Geza' With the background of the above scriptures for tsemach as indubitably referring to Christ, various LDS commentators and authorities have effectively equated tsemach and nêtser with what appears to be an innocent, unquestioned reliance on the capitalization of nêtser as Branch in Isaiah 11:1 by the KJV translators. Bruce R. Mc Conkie states, "Isaiah speaks of the Stem of Jesse, whom he also designates as a branch growing out of the root of that ancient worthy. He recites how the Spirit of the Lord shall rest upon him;

how he shall be mighty in judgment; how he shall smite the earth and slay the wicked; and how the lamb and the lion shall lie down together in that day - all of which has reference to the Second Coming and the millennial era thereby ushered in. (Isa. 11.) As to the identity of the Stem of Jesse, the revealed words says: "Verily thus saith the Lord: It is Christ." (D&C 113:1-2) This also means that the Branch is Christ, as we shall not see from other related scriptures." (Promised Messiah, Bruce R. Mc Conkie, p. 192) Then Elder Mc Conkie proceeds to cite and comment on three of the four abovementioned scriptures from Jeremiah and Zechariah that employ the word tsemach for branch and refer to Christ as the Branch without addressing the notion that Isaiah 11:1 actually employs the word nêtser instead. Ellis Rasmussen states, "Because Jesse was the father of King David and his descendants were the kings of Judah, of whose lineage Jesus would be born, it is clear who this 'Branch' is. Jesus the Lord, Jehovah; hence the wisdom, understanding, counsel, might, knowledge, and reverence of the Lord reside in him. Jeremiah and other prophets also knew this 'Branch' (cf. Jer. 23:5 and fn.) … Modern revelation also confirms that Jesus was the one anticipated (Isa. 11:1-5; D&C 113:1-2). His righteous reign on earth, bringing justice and peace to man and beast, will be established in his second coming (Isaiah 11:4-9)." (Ellis T. Rasmussen, A Latter-day Commentary on the Old Testament, p. 512) Rasmussen makes the similar assumption as did Elder Mc Conkie above. Doctrine and Covenants Student Manual states, in reference to Isaiah 11, "The branch and stem are both Christ (see Jeremiah 23:5-6; 33:15-17); these terms refer to Christ's being of the lineage of King David, the son of Jesse." (p. 283) Monte S. Nyman states, "The branch which is to grow out of the roots is Christ." ("Great Are the Words of Isaiah, Monte S. Nyman, p.71) KJV editors that capitalized Branch or BRANCH did not have the benefit of D&C 113:1-2. It appears LDS pundits followed the KJV translators' who incorrectly associated Isaiah's 11:1 nêtser with the other direct references to Christ as the tsemach. Save one narrow exception due to potential dual meaning (Isaiah 4:2), no other biblical use of nêtser ostensibly refers to Christ while all uses of tsemach do refer to Christ. Evidence Deduced from the Direct Parallelism of Isaiah Further, accepting at face value Rasmussen's proposition (above) that Isaiah 11:1 is a Hebrew direct parallel poetic couplet, one effectively equates or pairs chôter with nêtser (that is, rod and twig are paired with branch, shoot and descendant) and geza' with sheresh (that is, trunk, stump, stem, and stock are paired with roots). (See the table immediately below.) Such a direct comparison or parallelism is very reasonable, given the similarity of the definitions and synonyms. One is figuratively a relatively small outgrowth from a larger, fundamental part, tree, or entity. chôter geza' nêtser sheresh KJV rod stem of Jesse Branch roots Possible Strong alternatives rod twig trunk stump stem stock shoot descendant roots Conversely, a chiastic or inverted parallelism does not appear proper in this verse for the same reasoning as above and would be correspondingly much more difficult to infer. Further, accepting arguendo that Isaiah 11:1 is a direct parallelism and then inserting what is taught from D&C 113:1-2 that Christ is the stem of Jesse (or geza'), then one reaches the following comparison: We effectively equate chôter with nêtser (that is, rod and twig are paired with branch, shoot and descendant) and geza' and Christ are paired with sheresh (that is, trunk, stump, stem, and stock are paired with roots). The result with the replacements would read like as follows: "And there shall come forth a rod out of Christ, and a Branch shall grow out of Christ." Continuing the analysis, and assuming arguendo that Christ is also nêtser (the branch) (as suggested above by various scholarly and ecclesiastical authorities) (note, the author is not questioning that Christ

This paper analyzes, in turn, the etymological roots of, the meaning of, and interpretations of the key nouns in said scripture, namely rod, stem of Jesse, Branch (or branch), and roots. In contravention to various ecclesiastical and biblical commentaries, this paper proposes evidence and reasoning for a different interpretation of the word branch (as opposed to the Branch) as used in the context of Isaiah 11:1. Hebraic and Doctrinal Denotations Rod: Pursuant to The New Strong's Exhaustive Concordance of the Bible (hereinafter "Strong's"), the rod of Isaiah 11:1 is the Hebrew word chôter (reference number 2415) and means "from an unused root of uncertain significance; a twig - rod". Doctrine and Covenants (D&C) 113:3-4 provides additional insight as to the meaning of the rod. "What is the rod spoken of in the first verse of the 11th chapter of Isaiah, that should come out of the Stem of Jesse? Behold, thus saith the Lord: It is a servant in the hands of Christ, who is partly a descendant of Jesse as well as of Ephraim, or of the house of Joseph, on whom there is laid much power." Stem: Pursuant to Strong's, the stem of Isaiah 11:1 is the Hebrew word geza' (reference number 1503) and means "from an unused root meaning to cut down (trees), the trunk or stump of a tree (as felled or planted) - stem, stock." Stem of Jesse: D&C 113:1-2 gives the express, direct, and indisputable interpretation as to what the Stem of Jesse signifies. "Who is the Stem of Jesse spoken of in the 1st, 2d, 3d, 4th, and 5th verses of the 11th chapter of Isaiah? Verily thus saith the Lord: It is Christ." (As the Stem of Jesse is unquestionably Christ, it is noteworthy that verses 2 through 5 of Isaiah 11 have direct reference to Christ's power, glory, judgment, equity, righteousness and faithfulness.) Branch (or more specifically branch): Pursuant to Strong's, the branch of Isaiah 11:1 is the Hebrew word nêtser (reference number 5342) and means "in the sense of greenness as a striking color; a shoot; figuratively a descendant - branch." The KJV capitalizes this use of branch in Isaiah 11:1 which issue is discussed more at length below. Roots: Pursuant to Strong's, the roots of Isaiah 11:1 is the Hebrew word sheresh (reference number 8328) and means "a root (literal or figurative): - bottom, deep, heel, root." Different Word Choices and Interpreting Ellis Rasmussen's Literal Translation Substituting the four key Hebrew words above into KJV of Isaiah 11:1 reads as follows, "And there shall come forth a chôter out of the geza' of Jesse, and a nêtser shall grow out of his sheresh." Similarly, one may effectively substitute different words from those chosen by the KJV translators to generate a different and potentially superior translation of such text. While indicating that Isaiah 11:1 is a Hebrew direct poetic couplet, Ellis Rasmussen has done so and suggests the literal translation into common English is "There shall come forth a branch from the trunk of Jesse: Indeed, a shoot from his roots shall bear fruit". (Introduction to the Old Testament 2:45 as quoted in D&C Student Manual) If one then inserts the replacement for stem of Jesse (pursuant to D&C 113) into the above literal translation, the result is as follows: "There shall come forth a branch from Christ: Indeed, a shoot from his roots shall bear fruit". (italics added) Accepting Rasmussen's prima facie assertion that Isaiah 11:1 is a Hebrew direct poetic couplet, chôter and nêtser are parallel ideas while geza' of Jesse and sheresh are parallel ideas. Such direct parallelism can be readily seen from the table below. chôter geza' nêtser sheresh KJV Rod stem of Jesse Branch roots Rasmussen branch trunk shoot roots D&C 113 Christ Possible Strong alternatives rod twig trunk stump stem stock shoot descendant roots

is the Branch or tsemach), attempting to insert Christ as well for nêtser creates an inconsistency within the direct parallelism, as Christ cannot be the chôter and nêtser and geza' and sheresh simultaneously. The result with the replacements would oddly read like as follows: "And there shall come forth Christ out of Christ, and Christ shall grow out of Christ." The insertions of Christ for the various figurative paired Hebrew words are mutually exclusive. If one believes Isaiah 11:1 is a direct parallelism, then one cannot consistently rationalize that Christ is both geza' (as indubitably taught by the D&C 113:1-2) and nêtser (the branch; not the Branch (tsemach)). Again assuming a direct parallelism, believing such an inconsistent analysis where Christ is both geza' and nêtser would create a silly, meaningless, and even a result more difficult to rationalize. ("And there shall come forth Christ out of Christ, and Christ shall grow out of Christ.") Thus, Isaiah 11:1 cannot be a direct parallelism, have Christ signify geza' (the stem of Jesse) and be nêtser (the branch). Furthermore, the chôter (rod) out of the stem of Jesse is clarified by D&C 113:3-4 as "a servant in the hands of Christ, who is partly a descendant of Jesse as well as of Ephraim, or of the house of much power." The Old Testament Student Manual: 1 Kings - Malachi states, in reference to chôter, "This scripture seems to be a reference to the Prophet Joseph Smith and to Ephraim's leadership in the restoration of the last days." Other LDS scholars agree stating, "Sidney Sperry suggested that the rod or 'servant in the hands of Christ' referred to the Prophet Joseph Smith. It may also refer to another latter-day prophet yet to come forth." (Parry, Parry, and Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, pp. 47-48, emphasis in original) (See also Nyman, p. 71) Thus, if Isaiah 11:1 is a direct parallelism, such evidence also supplies a fatal blow to the erroneous conclusion that Christ is nêtser (the branch), because nêtser is paired with chôter, and chôter is not Christ (according to scripture, supra). Conclusion As the 8th Article of Faith teaches, we must accept scripture translated correctly as questioned truth. The Doctrine and Covenants clearly teaches that Christ is the Stem of Jesse (geza') of Isaiah 11:1. The Doctrine and Covenants clearly teaches that a servant of Christ is the rod (chôter) out of the Stem of Jesse (geza') of Isaiah 11:1. We can reasonably accept biblical scholars' teachings that Isaiah 11:1 is a direct parallelism or poetic couplet. A serious inconsistency is invited, however, by blindly accepting the KJV translators' capitalization of nêtser as Branch. Generally, the use of nêtser in the Bible does not allude to Christ. In contrast, many if not all other KJV uses of another Hebrew word tsemach indeed mean branch in English and represent an honorific title of Christ, therefore meriting capitalization. The author asserts that the capitalization of nêtser as Branch in the KJV Bible is a translation error and a capitalization error on which many scholarly and ecclesiastical authorities have reasonably but erroneously relied due to the confusion of the similar English translations (branch) of nêtser and tsemach. This paper simply submits that we put the 8th Article of Faith in action as it relates to nêtser of Isaiah 11:1.

6: “In Paradise, as everybody knows, all creatures lived together in peace. So too, in Zion when it is restored to the earth, the lion shall lie down with the lamb. God's other creatures are an important part of the picture of heaven. A marvelous statement by Joseph Smith on this subject gives us a flash of insight into an amazing future: ‘John learned that God glorified Himself by saving all that His hands had made, whether beasts, fowls, fishes or men; and He will glorify Himself with them.’ Brigham Young said: ‘The millennium consists in this, every heart in the Church and kingdom of God being united in one. . . . All things else will be as they are now, we shall eat, drink, and wear clothing. Let the people be holy . . . and filled with the Spirit of God, and every animal and creeping thing will be filled with peace; the soil of the earth will bring forth in its strength, and the fruits thereof will be meat for man.’” (Hugh Nibley, Approaching Zion, p. 13)

“Prophets have predicted and poets have sung, all down the ages, of a time to come when the earth would rest; when man would cease his inhumanity to man, when they would beat their swords into plowshares and their spears into pruning hooks; when nation would not rise against nation, neither would they learn war any more; when the animal kingdom would subsist solely upon the vegetable kingdom, when the lion would eat straw like the ox, the lion and the lamb lie down together and a little child lead them; when peace would spread her white wings over this planet and grim-visaged war, smoothing his wrinkled front, should sit at her feet and learn wisdom for a thousand years.” (Collected discourses 1868-1898, ed. by B.H. Stuy, vol. 4, Orson F. Whitney, Aug. 12 1894)

“When these days come, every thing will be in its place. Then man will not shed the blood of his fellow man, nor beast the blood of its fellow beast, nor fowl the blood of its fellow fowl; but the Spirit of the Lord will be poured out upon all flesh, the curse be taken from off the earth, when it will become an inheritance for the poor and the meek, when their (sic) will be peace thereon and good will towards man.” (William Phelps, The Evening and Morning Star, vol. 2, no. 13, June 1833, p. 102)

10: A careful reading of Romans 15:12 reveals that Paul refers to this Isaiah passage as having reference to Christ not Joseph Smith. This is natural because many of the early Apostles thought that the term “last days” referred to the time period in which they were living. Dr. Sidney B. Sperry has suggested that Paul was not correct in using this scripture to refer to Christ. “Examination of Romans 15:12 demonstrates that Paul was closely following the Septuagint LXX, Greek translation) text of Isaiah 11:10 rather than the Hebrew. As a matter of fact, the LXX version is only a paraphrase of the original Hebrew. We notice that the Greek version of Isaiah 11:1 translates the Hebrew text, ‘stem of Jesse,’ as the ‘root of Jesse’ and uses the same phrase in Isaiah 11:10. Of interest is the fact that the Greek word riza (root) is used in both verses to translate different Hebrew words. Paul would be quick to discern that the ‘root of Jesse’ of the LXX text of Isaiah 11:1-5 was the Christ. And when he observed that the phrase ‘root of Jesse’ was used again in verse 10, he would naturally assume that it, too, had reference to the Christ. Hence the reason for his quotation in Romans 15:12.’” (Book of Mormon Compendium, p. 227)

“Quite obviously the ‘root of Jesse’ is a man, a descendant of Jesse and Joseph (as the Lord explains), who seems to have a great mission to perform in connection with gathering the remnant of Israel, as explained in Isaiah 11:11-16. I suggest that the ‘rod’ of verse 1 and the ‘root of Jesse’ of verse 10 refer to the same man, Joseph Smith. If the ‘rod’ in D&C 113:4 is the ‘servant in the hands of Christ, who is partly a descendant of Jesse as well as of Ephraim, or of the house of Joseph’, note that in verse 6 he seems to be more closely defined as a ‘descendant of Jesse, as well as of Joseph, unto whom rightly belongs the priesthood, and the keys of the kingdom, for an ensign, and for the gathering of my people in the last days.’ (Italics added.) Who better fits the description of the words in italics than Joseph Smith (see D&C 27:12-13; 86:8-11; 110:1-16; 115:18-19)? He rightly holds the priesthood and its keys by

From this prophecy we learn: First: That the Lord would establish an ensign among the Gentiles. Second: That the Lord would set his hand again the second time to recover the remnant of His people. Third: It will be noted that when the Lord would set His hand "the second time" to gather Israel, it would be twofold in nature: (1) That He would "assemble the outcasts of Israel, and gather together the dispersed of Judah." (2) That this great movement would have its inception among the Gentiles, the Kingdom of Israel or Ephraim. Fourth: That "The envy also of Ephraim shall depart, and the adversaries of Judah shall be cut off: Ephraim shall not envy Judah, and Judah shall not vex Ephraim." (Le Grand Richards, Israel Do You Know, pp. 153-54.)

11: “The time has at last arrived when the God of Abraham, of Isaac, and of Jacob, has set His hand again the second time to recover the remnants of his people, which have been left from Assyria, and from Egypt, and from Pathros, and from Cush, and from Elam, and from Shinar, and from Hamath, and from the islands of the sea, and with them to bring in the fulness of the Gentiles, and establish that covenant with them, which was promised when their sins should be taken away. See Isaiah xi, Romans xi: 25, 26 and 27, and also Jeremiah xxxi: 31, 32 and 33. This covenant has never been established with the house of Israel, nor with the house of Judah, for it requires two parties to make a covenant, and those two parties must be agreed, or no covenant can be made. “Christ, in the days of His flesh, proposed to make a covenant with them, but they rejected Him and His proposals, and in consequence thereof, they were broken off, and no covenant was made with them at that time… “Thus after this chosen family had rejected Christ and His proposals, the heralds of salvation said to them, ‘Lo, we turn unto the Gentiles;’ and the Gentiles received the covenant, and were grafted in from whence the chosen family were broken off.” (Joseph Smith, History of the Church, vol. 1, p. 313)

“Isaiah's soul seemed to be on fire, and his mind wrapt in the visions of the Almighty, while he declared, in the name of the Lord, that it should come to pass in the last days that God should set His hand again the second time to recover the remnant of His people, assemble the outcasts of Israel, gather together the dispersed of Judah, destroy the tongue of the Egyptian sea and make men go over dry-shod, gather them to Jerusalem on horses, mules, swift beasts, and in chariots, and rebuild Jerusalem upon her own heaps; while, at the same time, the destroyer of the Gentiles will be on his way; and while God was turning the captivity of Israel, he would put all their curses and afflictions upon the heads of the Gentiles, their enemies, who had not sought to recover, but to destroy them, and had trodden them under foot from generation to generation. “At the same time the standard should be lifted up, that the honest in heart, the meek of the earth

among the Gentiles, should seek unto it; and that Zion should be redeemed and be built up a holy city, that the glory and power of God should rest upon her, and be seen upon her; that the watchman upon Mount Ephraim might cry—‘Arise ye, and let us go up unto Zion, the city of the Lord our God;’ that the Gentiles might come to her light, and kings to the brightness of her rising; that the Saints of God may have a place to flee to and stand in holy places while judgment works in the earth; that when the sword of God that is bathed in heaven falls upon Idumea, or the world,—when the Lord pleads with all flesh by sword and by fire, and the slain of the Lord are many, the Saints may escape these calamities by fleeing to the places of refuge, like Lot and Noah.” (Wilford Woodruff, History of the Church, vol. 6, p. 26)

The gathering of Israel, as the second exodus. The first exodus is always used as the model for the second exodus. And so the Lord says, I will set my hand again the second time to do what I did the first time, when I pulled them out of the Egypt, only this time I'm going to be pulling them from all over the world. So the second exodus is much larger. So, he starts mentioning where the people are this time, and they are not just in Egypt, which shall be left in Assyria. So to begin the second exodus, you'll notice in Section 28 of the Doctrine and Covenants, the Lord says, Joseph, you are Moses; Oliver, you are Aaron. As soon as those two words are said and the covenant is set up, baptisim is restablished. The exodus is on its way.
12: which to meet. I suppose that in the blistering heat of that July Sunday they sat on the tongues of their wagons and leaned against the wheels while the Brethren spoke. The season was late, and they were faced with a gargantuan and immediate task if they were to grow seed for the next season. But President Young pleaded with them not to violate the Sabbath then or in the future. The next morning they divided into groups to explore their surroundings. Brigham Young, Wilford Woodruff, and a handful of their associates hiked from their campground a little to the south of us, on past the ground where we are, and up the hill to the north of us. They climbed a dome-shaped peak, President Young having difficulty because of his recent illness. When the Brethren stood on the summit, they looked over this valley to the south of them. It was largely barren, except for the willows and rushes that grew along the streams that carried water from the mountains to the lake. There was no building of any kind, but Brigham Young had said the previous Saturday, "This is the place." The summit where they stood was named Ensign Peak out of reference to these great prophetic words of Isaiah: "And he [speaking of God] will lift up an ensign to the nations from far, and will hiss unto them from the end of the earth: and, behold, they shall come with speed swiftly" (Isaiah 5:26). "And he shall set up an ensign for the nations, and shall assemble the outcasts of Israel, and gather together the dispersed of Judah from the four corners of the earth" (Isaiah 11:12). There is some evidence to indicate that Wilford Woodruff took from his pocket a bandanna handkerchief and waved it as an ensign or a standard to the nations, that from this place should go the word of the Lord, and to this place should come the people of the earth. I think they may also on that occasion have spoken of the building of the temple, which today stands a few feet east of here, in fulfillment of the words of Isaiah: "And it shall come to pass in the last days, that the mountain of the Lord's house shall be established in the top of the mountains, and shall be exalted above the hills; and all nations shall flow unto it. "And many people shall go and say, Come ye, and let us go up to the mountain of the Lord, to the house of the God of Jacob; and he will teach us of his ways, and we will walk in his paths: for out of Zion shall go forth the law, and the word of the Lord from Jerusalem" (Isaiah 2:2-3). (Gordon B. Hinckley, in Conference Report, Oct. 1989, 68; or Ensign, Nov. 1989, 51-52)

“Rising above the Salt Lake Valley is a dome-shaped peak. Brigham Young saw it in a vision before the Saints left Nauvoo. He saw an ensign descend upon the hill and heard the voice of Joseph Smith say, ‘Build under that point … and you will prosper and have peace.’ “When Brigham Young first arrived in the valley, he immediately recognized the peak. On the morning of July 26, 1847, the men who would eventually comprise the new First Presidency, along with several members of the Twelve, climbed its slopes. “This small group of priesthood leaders gazed out upon the valley below. ‘This is where we will plant the soles of our feet,’ President Young said, ‘and where the Lord will place his name amongst his people.’ “As I now stand at Ensign Peak and see the valley below, I marvel at the foresight of that little group. These prophets, dressed in old, travel-worn clothes, standing in boots they had worn for more than a thousand miles, spoke of a millennial vision. It was both bold and audacious. It was almost unbelievable. “Here they were, almost a thousand miles from the nearest settlement to the east and almost eight hundred miles from the Pacific coast. They were in an untried climate. They had never raised a crop here. They had not built a structure of any kind. “They were exiles, driven from their fair city on the Mississippi into this desert region of the West. But they were possessed of a vision drawn from the scriptures and words of revelation: ‘And he shall set up an ensign for the nations, and shall assemble the outcasts of Israel, and gather together the dispersed of Judah from the four corners of the earth’ (Isa. 11:12).” (Gordon B. Hinckley, “Faith in Every Footstep: The Epic Pioneer Journey,” Ensign, May 1997, 64)

When he arrived here he said, "This is the right place." This very statement indicates that he would know the place when he saw it. He had seen Ensign Peak in vision. Two days after his arrival, with some of his brethren, he climbed the peak and there, in the words of Isaiah, unfurled "an ensign for the nations" (Isaiah 11:12). It may have only been a pocket handkerchief that was used, but it was indicative of a great millennial vision on the part of those leaders. I am convinced they saw not only 1847 but the planting and growth of this work through the years to come. Brigham Young dug his cane into the sun-baked soil and he noted where a temple should be built. That building was in fulfillment of the words of Isaiah: "And it shall come to pass in the last days, that the mountain of the Lord's house shall be established in the top of the mountains, and shall be exalted above the hills; and all nations shall flow unto it. "And many people shall go and say, Come ye, and let us go up to the mountain of the Lord, to the house of the God of Jacob; and he will teach us of his ways, and we will walk in his paths: for out of Zion shall go forth the law, and the word of the Lord from Jerusalem" (Isaiah 2:2-3). Further said Isaiah: "And he will lift up an ensign to the nations from far, and will hiss unto them from the end of the earth: and, behold, they shall come with speed swiftly" (Isaiah 5:26). ("A Great Heritage", Gordon B. Hinckley, CES Fireside, 2 February 1997)

For a moment, may I take you back 142 years when there was, of course, no tabernacle here, nor temple, nor Temple Square. On July 24, 1847, the pioneer company of our people came into this valley. An advance group had arrived a day or two earlier. Brigham Young arrived on Saturday. The next day, Sabbath services were held both in the morning and in the afternoon. 13: “We are from Ephraim. The Lord expects us, since we are the custodians of his gospel as restored in these latter days, according to my understanding, to extend the hand of friendship to Judah, because after all we are all descendants of the prophets Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and we come under the promises that through their descendants should all the nations of the earth be blessed. “I do not know how the enmity and the envy between Ephraim and Judah can disappear except that we of the house of Ephraim, who have the custody of the gospel, should lead out in trying to bring to this branch of the house of Israel the blessings of the restored gospel… “And it seems to me that the only way that the tribe of Judah can be sanctified to dwell in his presence forever and ever will be when we bring to them the gospel of the Lord Jesus Christ as the Savior promised them it would be brought in the latter days.” (LeGRand Richards, Conference Report, Oct. 1956, pp. 23-24 as taken from the 1981 Old Testament Institute Manual, p. 150)
adversaries of Judah --rather, "the adversaries from Judah"; those of Judah hostile to the Ephraimites
14: “’they shall fly upon the shoulders of the Philistines toward the West.’ We recognize the fulfillment of that prophecy in the founding of this Church by Joseph Smith, a lineal descendant of Abraham, Isaac and Jacob, who thus lifted the Ensign for the gathering of their descendants from their long dispersion among the nations. But a part of the fulfillment rests with the Gentiles. Their steamships, their railroads, their means of rapid transit and communication—these are ‘the shoulders of the Philistines,’ upon which the children of Ephraim have been and are being brought to the West, to the land of Zion, where the New Jerusalem is to rise, where the pure in heart will assemble, and the necessary preparation be made for the coming of the Lord in his glory. God works outside as well as inside his Church, and uses big things and little things for the accomplishment of his purposes.” (Orson F. Whitney, Conference Report, Oct. 1919, p. 69)
15: Israelites crossed the Red Sea on dry ground (Ex. 14:21-22), an event that anticipates the gathering in the last days, when no powerful nation, mortal tyrant, or army will stop the promised events of the gathering of the saints around God’s standard.” (Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, 124)

There shall be a second exodus, destined to eclipse even the former one from Egypt in its wonders. So the prophecies elsewhere (Ps 68:22; Ex 14:22; Zec 10:11). The same deliverance furnishes the imagery by which the return from Babylon is described (Isa 48:20, 21).
in the seven streams --rather, "shall smite it (divide it by smiting) into seven (many) streams, so as to be easily crossed"
